q: The oxygen (O2) released in photosynthesis comes from which reactant?
options: Water (H2O) — proved by Ruben & Kamen (1941) using 18O isotope
explanation: Ruben & Kamen (1941) used 18O isotope: when H2^18O was labeled, released O2 was ^18O2. O2 comes from water photolysis at PS-II, not from CO2 (which is fixed into glucose in dark reaction).

q: The 'Hill reaction' in photosynthesis refers to:
options: Light-dependent O2 evolution from isolated chloroplasts with an electron acceptor (Robert Hill, 1937)
explanation: Hill (1937) showed isolated chloroplasts release O2 in light with an artificial electron acceptor (Hill reagent) — even without CO2. Proved O2 evolution is a light reaction independent of CO2 fixation, and source is water.

q: In the Z-scheme (non-cyclic photophosphorylation), electron flows as:
options: Water → PS-II(P680) → PQ → cyt b6f → PC → PS-I(P700) → Fd → NADP+
explanation: Z-scheme: water (split at PS-II/P680) → PS-II → plastoquinone → cyt b6f → plastocyanin → PS-I(P700) → ferredoxin → NADP+ → NADPH. Produces ATP + NADPH + O2. 'Z' from zig-zag energy diagram.

q: Which photosystem has a reaction centre absorbing at 700 nm (far-red)?
explanation: PS-I = P700 (absorbs 700 nm, far-red). PS-II = P680 (absorbs 680 nm, red). Emerson Enhancement Effect (red + far-red synergistic) proved two photosystems. Mnemonic: P700=PS-I, P680=PS-II.

q: Cyclic photophosphorylation differs from non-cyclic because it:
options: Produces only ATP (no NADPH, no O2); electrons cycle back to PS-I
explanation: Cyclic = only PS-I; electrons return from ferredoxin → cyt b6f → PC → back to P700. Produces only ATP (no NADPH, no O2). Supplements ATP when Calvin cycle demand is high. Both occur in thylakoid, not stroma.

q: RuBisCO's oxygenase activity leads to which wasteful process?
options: Photorespiration (O2 binds instead of CO2; wastes energy, releases CO2; significant in C3 plants at high temp)
explanation: RuBisCO has dual activity: carboxylase (photosynthesis) + oxygenase (photorespiration). Photorespiration: O2 binds RuBP → phosphoglycolate → releases CO2, no ATP/sugar. Significant in C3 plants at high temp/high O2. C4/CAM suppress it.

q: The first stable product of CO2 fixation in C4 (Hatch-Slack) pathway is:
options: Oxaloacetate (4C) — PEPC fixes CO2 to PEP → oxaloacetate
explanation: C4: PEP carboxylase (PEPC) fixes CO2 to PEP(3C) → oxaloacetate(4C), first stable product. Converted to malate/aspartate, transported to bundle sheath, decarboxylated → CO2 enters Calvin. C3 first product = 3-PGA(3C).

q: Glycolysis, the first stage of respiration, occurs in:
options: Cytoplasm (cytosol) — anaerobic; glucose → 2 pyruvate + 2 ATP(net) + 2 NADH
explanation: Glycolysis (EMP) in cytoplasm, anaerobic. Glucose → 2 pyruvate + 2 ATP(net) + 2 NADH. Common first step of aerobic & anaerobic respiration. Krebs in mitochondrial matrix; ETS on inner membrane; Calvin in chloroplast stroma.

q: The Krebs (TCA) cycle occurs in which location and was elucidated by whom?
options: Mitochondrial matrix, by Hans Krebs (Nobel 1953); acetyl-CoA oxidised → CO2, NADH, FADH2, GTP
explanation: Krebs cycle in mitochondrial matrix; Hans Krebs (Nobel 1953). Acetyl-CoA + oxaloacetate → citrate; oxidised to release CO2, NADH, FADH2, GTP. Amphibolic (catabolic + anabolic). Glycolysis = Embden-Meyerhof in cytoplasm.

q: In the ETS of aerobic respiration, the final electron acceptor is:
options: Oxygen (O2) — at complex IV, O2 + e- + H+ → H2O; without O2, ETS stops
explanation: O2 is final e- acceptor at Complex IV (cytochrome c oxidase): O2 + 4e- + 4H+ → 2H2O. Without O2, ETS backs up → Krebs stops → only glycolysis + fermentation. NAD+/FAD are carriers; cyt c is intermediate.

q: Complete aerobic oxidation of one glucose yields approximately how many ATP (classical estimate)?
options: 36–38 ATP (glycolysis 2 + Krebs 2 + ETS from 10 NADH and 2 FADH2; classical P/O ratios NADH=3, FADH2=2)
explanation: Classical 36–38 ATP: glycolysis 2 ATP + 2 NADH; pyruvate oxidation 2 NADH; Krebs 2 ATP + 6 NADH + 2 FADH2. 10 NADH×3=30 + 2 FADH2×2=4 + 4 substrate = 38 (or 36 with glycerol-P shuttle). Modern ~30-32. BPSC/NCERT accept 36-38.

q: Ascent of sap (water transport) in tall trees is primarily explained by:
options: Transpiration-pull cohesion-tension (Dixon & Joly, 1894) — evaporation creates negative pressure; water cohesion maintains column
explanation: Transpiration-pull cohesion-tension (Dixon & Joly 1894) is accepted mechanism. Evaporation from leaves → negative pressure (tension) → water pulled up; cohesion (H-bonds) + adhesion maintain continuous column. Root pressure (A) is minor push (guttation). Xylem transport passive (no ATP).

q: Translocation of sucrose in phloem is explained by which hypothesis?
options: Pressure-flow (Münch) hypothesis — sucrose loaded at source → water enters → high turgor pushes to sink
explanation: Pressure-flow (Münch 1930): at SOURCE (leaf), sucrose actively loaded into sieve tubes → water enters osmotically → high turgor; at SINK (roots/fruits), sucrose unloaded → low turgor → sap flows source→sink. Bidirectional. Active loading uses ATP. Option A = xylem.

q: Stomata open when guard cells:
options: Take up K+ (followed by water) → become turgid → curve opens pore (blue light stimulates K+ influx)
explanation: Stomata open: guard cells pump K+ IN (ATP-driven) → water enters → turgid → curve outward → pore opens. Blue light stimulates K+ influx. ABA (option D) triggers K+ EFFLUX → flaccid → CLOSE (water stress). Options A,B = closing.

q: Loss of liquid water (with dissolved salts) from leaf margins at night/early morning during high humidity is:
options: Guttation — through hydathodes, driven by root pressure (positive); droplets contain dissolved minerals
explanation: Guttation: liquid water (with dissolved minerals, NOT pure) exits via hydathodes at leaf tips/margins. Driven by root pressure (positive hydrostatic) when transpiration low (night/high humidity). Transpiration = water VAPOUR (pure) via stomata, by transpiration pull (negative pressure), daytime.

q: Apical dominance (suppression of lateral buds by shoot tip) is caused by:
options: Auxin (IAA) — from shoot apex; cytokinin counteracts (promotes lateral buds)
explanation: Apical dominance caused by auxin (IAA) from shoot apical meristem (polar downward transport), inhibiting lateral buds. Decapitation removes auxin → lateral buds grow (bushier). Cytokinin ANTAGONISES auxin — promotes lateral bud growth. Pruning exploits this.

q: Gibberellin was discovered in connection with which plant disease?
options: 'Foolish seedling' (Bakanae) disease of rice — fungus Gibberella fujikuroi; Kurosawa (1926)
explanation: Gibberellin discovered via 'Bakanae' (foolish seedling) disease of rice — infected seedlings grow abnormally tall. Kurosawa (1926) showed fungus Gibberella fujikuroi extract caused elongation → gibberellic acid (GA3). Used for grape elongation, malting. Auxin = Went 1928.

q: Which is the ONLY gaseous plant hormone, responsible for fruit ripening?
options: Ethylene (C2H4) — simple gas; ripening (climacteric fruits: banana, mango, tomato), senescence, abscission
explanation: Ethylene (C2H4) only gaseous plant hormone. Promotes fruit ripening (climacteric fruits show respiratory burst), senescence, abscission. Ethephon/Ethrel release ethylene (FSSAI ≤100 ppm). 1-MCP blocks ethylene (extends shelf life). Others are non-gaseous.

q: Abscisic acid (ABA) is the 'stress hormone' because it:
options: Causes stomatal closure during water stress + induces seed/bud dormancy
explanation: ABA = 'stress hormone'. Under water stress, ABA triggers K+ efflux from guard cells → stomata close → reduces water loss. Also induces seed/bud dormancy, inhibits growth. Stomatal closure is its key drought-survival role. Despite name, abscission is ethylene's role.

q: The response of plants to relative day/night length (flowering) is called:
options: Photoperiodism (Garner & Allard, 1920) — critical factor is length of UNINTERRUPTED DARK period; SDP/LDP/DNP
explanation: Photoperiodism (Garner & Allard 1920): flowering response to day/night length. SDP (rice, soybean, chrysanthemum) flower in long nights; LDP (wheat, barley, spinach) in short nights; DNP (tomato, maize) unaffected. Critical factor = uninterrupted DARK period (night-break flash can prevent SDP flowering). Vernalisation = cold-induced flowering.

q: Vernalisation refers to:
options: Induction of flowering by exposure to low temperature (cold treatment) — winter wheat, biennials; Lysenko
Induction of flowering by long days
explanation: Vernalisation = induction of flowering by prolonged low temperature (0-5°C for weeks). Winter wheat, biennials (carrot, cabbage, beet) require cold to flower. Lysenko (USSR) developed it ('jarovisation'). Distinct from photoperiodism (temperature vs day length).

q: Makhana (Euryale ferox), major Bihar cash crop (Darbhanga, Madhubani, Purnia), is:
options: Nymphaeaceae (water lily family), aquatic C3 plant — Bihar >80% world; seeds popped; high protein
explanation: Makhana (Euryale ferox) = family Nymphaeaceae (water lily), AQUATIC C3 plant (no Kranz, no Hatch-Slack). Bihar >80% world production (Darbhanga, Madhubani, Purnia, Katihar). Seeds popped → edible, high protein, low fat. Makhana Research Centre Darbhanga (RPCAU). NOT a grass (Poaceae).

q: Which institution at Pusa, Samastipur (Bihar) is the state's premier agricultural university?
options: Dr. Rajendra Prasad Central Agricultural University (RPCAU/RAU), Pusa — originally Imperial Agricultural Research Institute (1905), shifted to Delhi 1936 (became IARI)
explanation: RPCAU (formerly RAU) at Pusa, Samastipur = Bihar's premier agri-university. Pusa was Imperial Agricultural Research Institute (1905); after 1934 Bihar earthquake, shifted to New Delhi 1936 → IARI. Pusa Bihar campus became RAU/RPCAU (named after Dr. Rajendra Prasad). ICAR-RCER Patna is separate.

q: Match hormone with function: 1.Auxin 2.Gibberellin 3.Cytokinin 4.Ethylene 5.ABA
explanation: 1.Auxin=elongation/phototropism/apical dominance. 2.Gibberellin=bolting(stem elongation)/seed germination. 3.Cytokinin=cell division/delays senescence. 4.Ethylene=fruit ripening/senescence/abscission(only gas). 5.ABA=stomatal closure(drought)/dormancy. Mnemonic: 'All Good Crops Eat Apples.'

q: CAM plants (pineapple, cacti) adapt to arid conditions by:
options: Opening stomata during day to maximise CO2
Using Calvin cycle only at night
Opening stomata at NIGHT (fix CO2 into malic acid); DAY: stomata close, malate decarboxylated → CO2 for Calvin cycle
explanation: CAM = temporal separation. Night: stomata OPEN (low transpiration) → CO2 fixed by PEPC into malic acid (stored in vacuole). Day: stomata CLOSE → malate decarboxylated → CO2 released → Calvin cycle (light provides ATP/NADPH). Minimises water loss. C4 uses spatial separation. High water-use efficiency, slow growth.
